video_id: YHrMWoIN5To
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YHrMWoIN5To
title: The Macro Minute: Is the markets dovish interpretation of the May CPI data correct?

Happy Wednesday out there, team 42. It's your skipper here, Darius Dale, with our macro minute for Wednesday, June 10th, 2026. As always, we start with the executive summary from today's lead-off morning note, so let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is, is the market's dovish interpretation of the May CPI data correct? The short answer is, from a short-term perspective, yes. From a medium-term perspective, no. In terms of the analytical nuance, the May CPI data supported the consensus outlook for peaking inflation. The May headline CPI, food CPI, and energy CPI data supported our sticky inflation theme. The May core CPI, core services CPI, and super core CPI data also supported our sticky inflation theme, as did the May shelter CPI data. Although statistical quirks are still impacting the shelter CPI time series, the second consecutive month of acceleration on both a 3-month and 6-month annualized basis is concerning and may represent early evidence of a departure from the lagged disinflation of nationwide rents. US dollar money markets had a dovish reaction to the May CPI report. There are currently 30 basis points of tightening priced into the next 12-month outlook, down four basis points day-over-day. Although it remains structurally elevated, the contribution to month-over-month headline CPI from core services declined sequentially, which takes some pressure off the Fed to tighten monetary policy. Although the contribution to year-over-year headline CPI from core services accelerated to an above-trend level of 208 basis points, this is likely within the range of what is considered acceptable by the median FOMC member. Despite the good news from the May CPI report, our market-implied Fed neutral rate model still sees the Fed as one to two rate hikes behind the curve, meaning it must hike by that amount to remove its accommodative bias and get back to neutral. Contrary to dovish hopium, the massive aggregate demand shock from AI CapEx is contributing to accelerating inflation via the a positive impulse in personal computers and peripheral equipment CPI. Can a central bank that is projecting to miss its own inflation target for seven consecutive years ignore today's massive aggregate demand shock to anchor on tomorrow's productivity boom? This is the known unknown with respect to the reaction function of the Kevin Warsh Fed. As always, we'll wrap up with a question from our community here in the 42 macro dashboard. Uh this one's titled lagging impacts of energy shortages. The best oil and commodity analysts have been sounding the alarm that there will be significant shortages and subsequent demand destruction as energy prices continue to move higher. Every day the street is closed seems to increase the impact of those events. My concern is Kitchin and Dr. Mo not built to model this lagging effect. I'm bullish on stocks for the reasons Diddy has so wonderfully communicated, but I'm I'm concerned that Kitchin and Dr. Mo models are not able to adequately account for this uh lagged effect. Uh so I'm just going to reply to the member with a reply that the other member posted here in the in the chat here. So I'm just going to read their reply. Uh says Kitchin and Dr. Mo are not designed to get us out at the very top, but to get us out when the trend changes, usually under 5% from the peak in the last few years. If the oil and commodity analysts are right, more more professional traders will realize this and will exit stocks pushing VT down and causing a regime phase transition or switch VT from bullish VAMs to neutral VAMs or bearish VAMs. So this person is highlighting the top-down overlay in Kitchin and Dr. Mo which incorporates the market regime which factors the vol targeting which which pulls the vol targeting components into for each asset class. And then the bottom-up risk management overlay in Kitchin and Dr. Mo is the actual volatility adjusted momentum signal condition of the asset itself being bullish, neutral, or bearish. And so those two and then the the VAM signal, you know, brings in the dynamic a sizing component of the risk management process. So, when you add vault targeting from a top-down perspective and dynamic position sizing from a from a bottom-up perspective, you wind up producing a positively skewed return distribution, which allows your portfolio to compound returns across higher net asset values, which allows your wealth growth to compound faster across multiple market cycles. This is one of the you know, not so secrets of of institutional finance and then part of the reason why the entire hedge fund industry exists. We understand that the sequence of returns and then and chopping off left tail left tail risk eliminating type two errors and chopping off left tail risk is the is the key to compounding wealth faster than you otherwise would as a just a ordinary buy and hold investor. And so, the last thing I'll say is that you know, Kiss and Dr. Mo don't factor in any fundamental analysis. You know, when you talk about the growth cycle, the inflation cycle, monetary policy, fiscal policy, liquidity, and positioning. Those are the six key macro cycles that matter in terms of influencing the momentum and dispersion within and across asset markets. Kiss and Dr. Mo are not designed to key on those factors because those factors are have a jagged edge of data reporting that ultimately causes latency with respect to financial market developments. Kiss and Dr. Mo are designed to anchor on price and volatility data, the same price and volatility data that feed into the global macro risk matrix to produce the market regime that takes about, you know, 700 ish you know, time series analyzed on a daily basis to generate our market regime determination. And then we have another about 800 time series on a daily basis that are analyzed to produce the Dr. Mo and Kiss signals. So, on on a daily basis, every single day when we refresh our models, we're analyzing about 1,500 time series to generate the exposures and the proper trade signals that are featured in Kiss and Dr. Mo and those are forward-looking and they compress the latency relative to things like you know, energy supply shocks or or the other stuff that you need fundamental data to uh um to to to determine the outcome for. So, uh hopefully that explanation was helpful. Uh just be calm comfortable and calm, cool, and collected. If we're about to head into a market crash, trust that risk management overlords and kissing Dr. Mo will get us out near the top. You're never going to sell the exact top. If you optimize your investment process for selling the exact top or buying the exact bottom, you will commit a lot of type two errors. And the type two errors are the kinds of errors that take you out uh behind the woodshed and shoot your portfolio in the head. So, I'll leave you with that. I'll leave you with that um that that that visual because that's exactly the kind of visual you need to be left with to understand why all of Wall Street is designed to eliminate type two errors. Not all, but most most of most most institutional investors are designed to eliminate type two errors, and this is why we get paid the big bucks. So, we're wrapping up there. Derry Stern here presenting our Marakon Minute for Wednesday, June 10th, 2026. Best of luck out there today. We'll catch you back here tomorrow. Cheers.
